The context (and socket) should be created in `run`, not in `__init__`. `__init__` is called in the parent process, only `.run` is called in the forked process.

In more complex applications, if it gets hairy and you have multiple sockets you do want "equal" asyncio scheduling. Sockets which have a lot of data traffic should be scheduled more often (otherwise performance is impacted), so the question is what should happen when everyone gets a lot of data.

That is why I think a size based starvation algorithm would be better for throughput based applications and a message count based starvation algorithm would be better for highly transactional applications. Adding both options so people can choose would not be bad.

The current approach for time based starvation will probably be detrimental for highly transactional applications.

I am not sure if I got your question, but I'll give it a try. 
You could config the database to accept SSL enabled, http-only connections.

Say you has a port 1234, you could config it to open for ssl-enabled, http-only connections by using the following settings in a Neo4j database:

```
dbms.connector.1234.address=localhost:1234
dbms.connector.1234.enabled=true
dbms.connector.1234.encryption=TLS
dbms.connector.1234.type=HTTP
```

If you got another port 4321, and you want to config it differently, then just set it accordingly, such as:

```
dbms.connector.4321.address=localhost:4321
dbms.connector.4321.enabled=true
dbms.connector.4321.encryption=REQUIRED
dbms.connector.4321.type=BOLT
```

Then you explicitly set what kind of connections that you want for different ports.
For more info: http://neo4j.com/docs/operations-manual/3.0/#_configuring_bolt_connectors

Hope this answered your questions :)

Hi uganjha,
Thank you for your inquiry into the reconnect API.

Here is a pointer to the [HostSystem](http://pubs.vmware.com/vsphere-65/index.jsp#com.vmware.wssdk.apiref.doc/vim.HostSystem.html) API.
```
ReconnectHost_Task(reconnect)

Reconnects to a host. This process reinstalls agents and reconfigures the host, if it has gotten out of date with VirtualCenter. The reconnection process goes through many of the same steps as addHost: ensuring the correct set of licenses for the number of CPUs on the host, ensuring the correct set of agents is installed, and ensuring that networks and datastores are discovered and registered with VirtualCenter.
The client can change the IP address and port of the host when doing a reconnect operation. This can be useful if the client wants to preserve existing metadata, even though the host is changing its IP address. For example, clients could preserve existing statistics, alarms, and privileges.

This method can also be used to change the SSL thumbprint of a connected host without disconnecting it.

Any changes made to the resource hierarchy on the host when the host was disconnected are overriden by VirtualCenter settings on reconnect.

This method is only supported through VirtualCenter.

Required Privileges
Host.Config.Connection
Parameters
NAME	TYPE	DESCRIPTION
_this	ManagedObjectReference	A reference to the HostSystem used to make the method call.
cnxSpec*	HostConnectSpec	
Includes the parameters to use, including user name and password, when reconnecting to the host. If this parameter is not specified, the default connection parameters is used. 
reconnectSpec*	HostSystemReconnectSpec	
Includes connection parameters specific to reconnect. This will mainly be used to indicate how to handle divergence between the host settings and vCenter Server settings when the host was disconnected.
Since vSphere API 5.0
```

So you can call it with ReconnectHost_Task.

The `__import__('__main__'_)` case can be unpredictable across many projects.  Users of projects like [__python-fire__](https://github.com/google/python-fire), [__doit__](http://pydoit.org) and [__luigi__](luigi.readthedocs.io/en/stable/command_line.html) could get value from __MonkeyType__.  These applications and interactive computing contexts generally have a lower code and packaging dependency.  In larger software projects, the consequence of`__import__('__main__'_)` is generally unpredictable.

It would rad if `__main__` could work with the `list-modules` machinery.  `__main__` is likely a special case because it behaves unpredictably compared to normal packages.  Maybe an argument like `list-main` which appends `__main__` to `list-modules` would work.

In IPython, this configuration could be done as an [IPython extension]() similar to the way the [`%%monkey`](http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/deathbeds/XXX/blob/master/XXX/monkey.ipynb#IPython-magic.) magic was created.

I'd like to see VLAN support to as an actual "object" type too, just some general thoughts from our use cases (as a service provider):

VLANs are a finite resource so we need to track their usage and who they are allocated to (they can run out like IPs!).

We need to be able to allocate a VLAN to a device and it is a device wide VLAN allocation (a broadcast domain, perhaps multiple ports on a single device are access ports in the same VLAN), or at the interface level assign a VLAN but actually be able to re-use the same VLAN across multiple interfaces or devices within a single PoP and it is not a device wide broadcast domain (in the instance that traffic comes into interface 1 tagged with VLAN 10 and we pop the VLAN tag off and place the traffic then into broadcast domain/VLAN 20 for switching, traffic also comes into interface 2 tagged on VLAN 10 but we pop the tag there and place the traffic into VLAN 30 for switching, so both interfaces have traffic tagged with VLAN 10 but they are unrelated).

If we are providing a layer 2 service (like a pseudowire) between two PoPs we might want to allocate the same VLAN In both PoPs (but only to a specific interface at each PoP) so we need to search PoPs and/or devices if VLAN 10 is free in both places.

HTH.

As I've commented earlier, the problem is that there are cases when a locator matches multiple elements but SL just throws other than the first one away.  The place where it happens is in [ElementFinder.find()](https://github.com/robotframework/SeleniumLibrary/blob/master/src/SeleniumLibrary/locators/elementfinder.py#L80). This feels pretty random for meand test automation shouldn't be random. It ought to always be possible to construct a locator that matches exactly the desired element. For example, instead of using `//h1` you ought to be able to use `//h1[1]`.

This mostly affects keywords interacting with elements. For example, if I say `Click Link    class:xxx` and there are multiple links with class `xxx`, blindly clicking the first link sounds wrong. Notice that jQuery would click all links in this case, but I don't think that's a good idea with SL. Requiring users to give an exact locator sounds much better.

I was wrong above when I validation keywords wouldn't be affected in general. It's true that `Contains` keyword shouldn't be affected, but keywords like `Element Text Should Be` probably are. My guess is that at the moment they also only check the first element and ignore others. I can see many ways this could create false positive results.

The main problem with the change is that it is backwards incompatible and likely requires many users to updated their locators. Although updating should generally be pretty easy, there may be lot of locators to update making the change problematic. We definitely cannot make the situation error right a way but emitting a warning ought to be fine. If there a lot of complaints, the warning can then be removed.

The current concept of pipenv relies on the [``which`` command](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/f3075073713b6ae9ae6e07849a056a40e015db73/pipenv/core.py#L384). This command allows not only to find the first executable that matches the version, but could return *all* executables that matches the version. Together with your "version check code snippet" it would be possible to check all found executables for their bitness and take the matching one (if available).

But I am not sure if there is a "standard" way of installing python side by side on linux (After googling around I have the feeling that multiple python architectures on a single machine [is problematic](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30663012/installing-python-32-bit-on-64-bit-kubuntu)). Thus I could imagine that *if* somebody installs boths architectures side by side, the 64bit installation is done via the paketmanager, while the 32bit installation is done manually and thus might even be not present in the PATH (in which case pipenv would not find the interpreter at all)

If this is true, it might be enough to simply check if the bitness of the found interpreter is OK on linux and macOS, while on windows (where side by side is a usual case) the discussed solution is used to select the correct one.

An interesting comparison, in the Docker context, is described here [Builder pattern vs. Multi-stage builds in Docker](https://blog.alexellis.io/mutli-stage-docker-builds/) by Alex Ellis

It was compelling in the Docker case for reasons beyond just configuration, and was implemented as [Docker MultiStage Builds](https://docs.docker.com/engine/userguide/eng-image/multistage-build/)

The discussion is here:
- https://github.com/docker/docker/pull/32063
- https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/31067
- https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/7149

Docker configs go to product/published image sizes and caching efficiency.

That said, it helps in configuration management as well, and as an example of a configuration file that imports dependencies instead of requiring complex and fragile builder routines.

How about a find_packages that works correctly on python 3.3+, but falls back to current behaviour for older pythons? Legacy find_packages can still work as is for older packages. 

The problem with adding a separate namespace_packages function, or a separate argument is that I don't think people will know to look for them. How do users figure out the reason their package that works in python doesn't work when packaged?

Common folders like doc, and virtualenv ones can be removed from consideration by find_packages by looking for things like 'site-packages' and common sphinx files. Note, that we already have an exclude argument in find_packages.

Is there a tool to test setuptools changes over a wide range of packages already? I would be nice to base decisions on data. I'd be happy to do the work to find out if filtering out false positives will be effective.

When people have a sample/sample.py which works as a package in python, and then doesn't with packaging, we have a major packaging wart.

If find_packages is not going to be fixed, perhaps we can stop recommending to use it in the docs, and instead recommend a 'new_find_packages' or whatever a new api could be called?

At first blush, a callback after `_rebalance` sounds like it wouldn't be too problematic to implement. As long as we kept it very generic and didn't try to give the callback too much information about any state, I think it would be fine. Sounds like a worthwhile pull request.

I'm wondering about your use case. Does each consumer have its own separate in-memory data structure it's responsible for modifying, or are all of the consumers pushing updates to a single structure? If you want all updates to go to the same structure, it seems like you could use a single consumer or a group of balanced consumers without any issue. If you're modifying multiple structures (one per consumer), I think separate consumer groups would be more suited to your use case.

It's extremely likely I'm misunderstanding something about your use case - please enlighten me!

@carsonip Thanks for contributing this issue report - it will be very helpful as we improve pykafka's support for greenlets. The short answer to why the producers you're using occasionally freeze up is that pykafka's gevent support is not battle-tested to the same degree that the threaded version is. Using a single producer instead of multiple is a good way to minimize the likelihood of this blocking behavior manifesting. In theory it shouldn't be a problem to use multiple producers in parallel greenlets, but in practice the current version of the library can have some trouble with this.

According to the document : http://storm.apache.org/documentation/Clojure-DSL.html

A stream grouping can be one of the following:

    :shuffle: subscribes with a shuffle grouping
    Vector of field names, like ["id" "name"]: subscribes with a fields grouping on the specified fields
    :global: subscribes with a global grouping
    :all: subscribes with an all grouping
    :direct: subscribes with a direct grouping

in details:  http://storm.apache.org/documentation/Concepts.html
Stream groupings

Part of defining a topology is specifying for each bolt which streams it should receive as input. A stream grouping defines how that stream should be partitioned among the bolt's tasks.

There are seven built-in stream groupings in Storm, and you can implement a custom stream grouping by implementing the CustomStreamGrouping interface:

1.Shuffle grouping: Tuples are randomly distributed across the bolt's tasks in a way such that each bolt is guaranteed to get an equal number of tuples.
 
2.Fields grouping: The stream is partitioned by the fields specified in the grouping. For example, if the stream is grouped by the "user-id" field, tuples with the same "user-id" will always go to the same task, but tuples with different "user-id"'s may go to different tasks.

3.Partial Key grouping: The stream is partitioned by the fields specified in the grouping, like the Fields grouping, but are load balanced between two downstream bolts, which provides better utilization of resources when the incoming data is skewed. This paper provides a good explanation of how it works and the advantages it provides.

4.All grouping: The stream is replicated across all the bolt's tasks. Use this grouping with care.

5.Global grouping: The entire stream goes to a single one of the bolt's tasks. Specifically, it goes to the task with the lowest id.

6.None grouping: This grouping specifies that you don't care how the stream is grouped. Currently, none groupings are equivalent to shuffle groupings. Eventually though, Storm will push down bolts with none groupings to execute in the same thread as the bolt or spout they subscribe from (when possible).

7.Direct grouping: This is a special kind of grouping. A stream grouped this way means that the producer of the tuple decides which task of the consumer will receive this tuple. Direct groupings can only be declared on streams that have been declared as direct streams. Tuples emitted to a direct stream must be emitted using one of the [emitDirect](/javadoc/apidocs/backtype/storm/task/OutputCollector.html#emitDirect(int, int, java.util.List) methods. A bolt can get the task ids of its consumers by either using the provided TopologyContext or by keeping track of the output of the emit method in OutputCollector (which returns the task ids that the tuple was sent to).
 
8.Local or shuffle grouping: If the target bolt has one or more tasks in the same worker process, tuples will be shuffled to just those in-process tasks. Otherwise, this acts like a normal shuffle grouping.

I generally agree, but I think that it makes much more sense for an app than it does for a library like this one that's used as a dependency. By constraining stripe-python's dependencies too tightly, we increase the likelihood that some other library in a user's app that also needs Requests will have conflicting version requirements and lead to transitive dependency hell.

I'll certainly reconsider if it turns out that this leads to serious problems, but given that the version bumps in Requests have been patch releases that were pushed relatively quickly after the breaking version, the prevalence of bad versions (2.14.0 and 2.14.1) should be relatively low.

Regarding private member:  The idea is to make the data structure harder to corrupt. In v1.x, ```G.node[n]``` was writeable. The node datadict it returned was also read-write and we want to keep that functionality. But we want to protect the outer dict in the dict-of-dict structure. To write the outer-dict use ```G._node```. where the private member warns you that you are doing funky things.

I would prefer to describe what changed as ```G.node``` became ```G.nodes```.  This change happened because the reporting of node data via the new dict-set object ```G.nodes``` encompasses everything ```G.node``` used to provide (except write-able outer dict).  In a sense, ```G.node```, ```G.nodes_iter``` and ```G.nodes``` all became one object ```G.nodes```

We are open for suggestions on this.

There's a bunch of stuff going on in the example.

First, in some cases multiprocessing can create multiple copies of your data. I would like you to try `list(map())` instead of `pool.map` to make sure that multiprocessing is "innocent" here. ;)

Second, we *always* use floating point images during transformations, in order to ensure accuracy during the interpolation process. So converting the images is no use, we will convert them back right under your nose. This can cause problems but there is little hope of changing things in the short term. Assuming your images are uint8 data, this already accounts for an 8x increase in RAM usage, to 9.6Go.

Third, depending on the transformation, it's possible that we are generating *all* output and input coordinates, which would again multiply the data size (@stefanv can you confirm this?). This we are working to reduce with an overhaul of the Transforms module: https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/3148

@max-arnold https://travis-ci.org/yourlabs/django-cities-light/jobs/168616978

indeed, ETags can be any string you like, some websites even use them to effectively cookie you without sending you a cookie (they send a different ETag to each visitor, and can then track you by looking at the `If-None-Match` header your browser sends, very sneaky).

Yes, in the remoteStorage spec `If-Match` and `If-None-Match` are also optional, one reason is that if you do GET for the first time, then you don't have any last-seen version yet to refer to, so you need to retrieve the document for sure, and the other reason is that they are optional in http, so making them required would be considered 'subsetting http', which is not best practice. For instance, in the latest versions of remoteStorage (not in earlier ones) we require servers to support all of http/1.1 (including chunked-transfer uploads), so that we can say we're not subsetting http.

It's indeed nice to keep sending a `Last-Modified` header, also for debugging, but then with the human-readable format, rounded off to whole seconds.

When Github merges PRs, it signs the merge commits with a GPG key it owns. When Bors merges a PR, it does so with this API: https://developer.github.com/v3/repos/merging/

That API method means that Bors doesn't have to clone our repositories and perform git merges itself, which is a huge enabling factor. Unfortunately, that API is very limited: it doesn't support squash and rebase merges, for example, and it won't create signed commits.

I don't think there is a bug on file for this because any feature that would require Bors cloning repositories has been treated as a non-started in the past by the developer.

Adding global message handling isn't the worst idea, but what @JoshuaGross is saying here is correct. 

We actually have a global message callback handler in the Python library, so we should actually just have one in the C library as well. The implications of adding a new concept of virtual message handling as a concept through a protected msg_type value is a substantial conceptual one. (not that new concepts are bad, just that we've typically discussed changes like this more in the past). Quickly looking at https://github.com/swift-nav/libsbp/blob/master/c/src/sbp.c, it does indeed seem like a tricky, but not impossible change.

OK, there is a `numpy.lib.NumpyVersion` function that is available when you import `numpy`.

Required changes are quite limited:

```patch
--- a/numexpr/expressions.py
+++ b/numexpr/expressions.py
@@ -18,7 +18,7 @@ import numpy
 # numpy's behavoir sometimes changes with versioning, especially in regard as 
 # to when ints are cast to floats.
 # _np_version will be similar to ('1', '13', '1') for which we can use simple comparisons
-_np_version = tuple( ver for ver in numpy.__version__.split('.') )
+_np_version = numpy.lib.NumpyVersion(numpy.__version__)
 
 # Declare a double type that does not exist in Python space
 double = numpy.double
@@ -283,7 +283,7 @@ def rtruediv_op(a, b):
 
 @ophelper
 def pow_op(a, b):
-    if (_np_version >= ('1', '12', '0b1') and
+    if (_np_version >= '1.12.0b1' and
         b.astKind in ('int', 'long') and
         a.astKind in ('int', 'long') and
         numpy.any(b.value < 0)):
```

Also the slowdown at import time should be quite limited

```python
%timeit np_version = np.lib.NumpyVersion(np.__version__)
The slowest run took 7.11 times longer than the fastest. This could mean that an intermediate result is being cached.
100000 loops, best of 3: 6.69 µs per loop

%timeit np_version = tuple( ver for ver in np.__version__.split('.') )
The slowest run took 8.66 times longer than the fastest. This could mean that an intermediate result is being cached.
1000000 loops, best of 3: 1.02 µs per loop
```

The main drawback IMO is that you need numpy >= 1.9 (4 years old) instead of numpy >= 1.7 that is the current requirement.

That seems like something that will be hard to deploy. The server has to keep track of all past resources and their client side cacheability in order to know which dictionary to use. Alternatively, you could send the image file as `multipart/form-data`, either using [HybridImageField](https://github.com/Hipo/drf-extra-fields/blob/master/drf_extra_fields/fields.py#L102) or the standard DRF `ImageField`.
But with `Base64ImageField` you need to encode your image file with *base64* before sending it as part of a JSON object. This will increase the file-size by about 30%, so if bandwith is an issue you might consider sending the request as `multipart/form-data` instead of JSON that includes a base64 encoded file.

Regarding what is best practice or not, I found little relevant stuff. However, at one place it was correctly stated that enabling compression is much more important than whether one pretty prints the json or not. 

Using `flask-compress` and adding to my `create_app`

```python
compress = Compress()
compress.init_app(app.app)
```

that is quickly done and indeed had a much greater impact than not pretty printing. 

The same source also argued as gdb from #193 - API results should be nice to look at for developers without any hassle.

So, I would rather close my own PR and go with compression, unless you make a strong point against that.

mon opinion :
1/ quelles que soient les règles adoptées, il faut qu'elles soient vérifiées par un outil, parce que sinon il y aura toujours une violation qui passera au travers
2/ cet outil devrait aussi vérifier les règles de manière automatique
  - par le CI : mise en échec lors d'une violation
  - par une intégration aux éditeurs de code les plus courants : par une notification de violation
  - par un pre-commit hook, pour éviter de commiter une violation par oubli ou mégarde
3/ cet outil serait idéalement exécuté sur chaque sauvegarde d'un fichier : l'avantage est que le formatage devient un non-problème. On code, on sauvegarde, ça se reformate, on ne s'en soucie plus. Ce sont les méthodes préconisées par gofmt, rustfmt, prettier, elm-format etc... par le biais d'une intégration à l'éditeur. De cette manière un nouveau contributeur n'a pas besoin d'apprendre le formatage spécifique à ce repository, un outil le fait automatiquement pour lui
4/ les règles adoptées devraient être la référence pour une communauté donnée. Pour reprendre les exemples ci-dessus, la configuration pour ces auto-formatteurs est minime (voire nulle) afin de supprimer toute différence entre différentes bases de code. J'ai l'impression que black est en train de devenir la référence pour la communauté Python, comme prettier l'est devenu pour JS, elm-format pour elm, rustfmt pour rust...

Pour l'outil black, ce qui me paraît pratique c'est qu'il fait le formatage automatique, et que sa configuration est minime (et je serai pour qu'il n'y en ai pas, pour garder la config par défaut).

I had another chance to look at this. I think a good strategy would be to first create a pull request that implements this only for totally safe operations such as reshape, nobackprop, and flip_gradient. Then we can think about what to do next. I see two paths, the first easier and the second more interesting:

1. Allow in-place operations. The pre-condition for doing this is that it doesn't add significant code complexity (over the implementation for reshape, nobackprop, and flip-gradient), and is not able to fail silently.
2. Allow execution-time operation fusing. This would mean that we could change ExecutionEngine a bit so it would detect which operations could be fused together to save memory and execution speed. I don't think that we should force the user to do this, but it'd be good if the backend could discover these opportunities for optimization automatically. One difficulty here is that we'd have to be sure that the intermediate nodes in the operations would not be used later when we expand the graph further. We could be sure of this by adding some capacity for reference counting, and only fuse nodes if there are no references to the intermediate nodes. Needless to say, this is a big job, but I think it'd very much in the spirit of what DyNet is trying to do (not bother the user with the details of optimizing for efficiency), so it might be an interesting project.

It's the module dict detection. Since the `__main__` dict is effectivly being pickled, `D1` is used and the pickling happens by reference, which then means that nothing is actually saved. You could turn `pickler._session` on as a fix, or change `__name__` to something other that `__main__`. Interestingly, doing `dic = locals().copy()` also fixes the problem as the resulting dict does not contain `dic` and so fails the checks.

>  How can I write a unit test along the lines of: ... (I want to add general tests for sources hydration which cover these cases: 8c8e6f0 but can't quite work out what the execution request I'm trying to craft is... Ideally this would be a unit test, not an integration test.) I'm specifically trying to write a test which exercises both the extracting and the hydrating of a SourcesField.

There are two unit test APIs for "creating targets" on `python.pants_test.test_base.TestBase`: 1) `self.add_to_build_file` and 2) `self.make_target`. The former will trigger the full pipeline of parsing in the engine and then injection into the graph... the latter will synthetically create a target in memory and then inject it into the graph.

The relationship between these two methods is quite similar to the relationship between what happens when we parse a target from a BUILD file vs what happens when we inject a target during codegen (respectively).

So, I think there are two options:

1. use `self.add_to_build_file` and (optionally) strip away some of the boilerplate around that
2. deprecate or overhaul `self.make_target` to use more of the engine codepath, rather than directly injecting into the BuildGraph.

More generally, the long term goal is to remove `BuildGraph.inject_target` entirely in favor of using `@rule`s and variants/subsystems for codegen and optional target creation, respectively. So it would seem to me that bias'ing toward cleaning up API 1 would be reasonable.

My opinion on this is that having this first argument as a single URI (local, remote or *special*) is quite nice. However, something experience taught me is that remote URIs **never** last. So, I wonder how the code would cope with say a change of URI for the location of the special data.

As far as @blink1073 suggestion is concerned, let me quote [PEP 20](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/) here:

> Explicit is better than implicit.

But that's just my opinion.

Besides, from a pure functional standpoint, you'd end up with one function having multiple responsibilities (reading AND fetching). That's why in all honesty, I would prefer having a separate `imageio.fetch` function, which would abstract management of remote content and return a URI compatible with `imageio.imread`.
BTW: imageio will very likely soon wrap Pillow to support the "common" formats. If Pillow can read the kind of images that you're using, it might be easier to wait for that ...

Hi @ghcs27,
this looks good - I have often thought of adding it, rather than writing it in my own code.

Only suggested addition is a little more documentation to help prevent questions - something like:
```
Smoothly change volume
      
from the current volume to the desired value. Volume can be changed up or down.
The ramp rate is linear at 1.25 steps per second. For example: to change from 
volume 50 to volume 30 would take 16 seconds. This rate is selected by Sonos.
This method is non blocking and has no network overhead once sent.
```
There are other Ramp Types but I don't think it is worth making them available.
`ALARM_RAMP_TYPE` resets to zero, waits about 30secs, then ramps at 2.5 steps per second !
`AUTOPLAY_RAMP_TYPE` resets to zero then ramps very quickly at 50 steps per second.

I would vote to ignore these and leave code functionality as you have it. What do others think?
Cheers David

Dear Wyatt,

Thank you for the feedback! 

Regarding your question related to the (+) and (-) suffixes for the regulons: this is experimental work. The (+) indicates that there is a positive correlation between the expression levels of a TF and its target genes across cells (from which we infer that the regulon is a transcriptional activator), while the (-) indicates the positive, i.e. a transcriptional inactivating relationship between the TF and its targets.
This is work in progress. The (+) labelled regulons are the ones you would get from running the original R version.

If you want to filter based on direct or indirect TF annotations you can easily do so by first filtering the dataframe with enriched motifs [i.e. df = prune2df()] and only subsequently derive regulons from the df using df2regulons. You should focus on the column "Annotation" which should contain "gene is directly annotated" for direct annotations. More fine grained control is possible by also using the columns "MotifSimilarityQValue" (should be 0 if no motif similarity is needed to find a matching TF annotation for the enriched motif in the species under investigation) and "OrthologousIdentity" (a value between 0.0 and 1.0 which signifies the orthologous identity of the DBD of involved TF proteins when SCENIC needs to cross species boundaries to find an appropriate annotation for the enriched motifs). 

Hope this helps,
Bram

This error message typically means you have defined the incorrect port and possibly the incorrect host as well.  As the error message says, you have defined a host and port that is for the SAS Metadata Server.  SASPy is looking for a connection to your SAS Workspace Server.  The default port for this is 8591.  Your winiomwion configuration has 8561, which is the default for Metadata, so the simplest thing to do would be to try 8591 instead.  If your Workspace server is not on the same host as your Metadata server, the host would also require an update.

I see a couple issues here. 
1. GDEF and mark classes have not been generated correctly
2. Zeroing of mark width (like Glyphs does by default on export). This is also addressed in the above pull request.
3. Kerning. Glyphs export makes a features.fea file with a kern feature. In the UFOs generated by pipeline the features.fea file does not contain the kerning at all. It is only in the UFO's kerning.plist, but does not seem to be applied correctly. I tried copying features.fea from the Glyphs export into the UFO and then running fontmake -u on the UFO and the spacing and kerning was then correct. Also, the Glyphs generated font has the kern feature under both hebr/default and hebr/IWR, but pipeline only has it under hebr/default and not DFLT/default. I don't think that would be easy but OTOH the whole thing is weird. It looks like it's there in same someone (the user?) SIGSTOP-ed ebd, I don't see any code in pkgcore that would have caused that to happen but then, I used only simple grep.

If we were to reimplement this, we'd have to look at how processes are managed. Possible way of doing this (at least on some systems) would be catching `SIGCHLD` and then using `waitpid()` with `WCONTINUED`, and then checking for `WIFSTOPPED` and `WIFCONTINUED`. Now, I don't know exact portability details on the two.

POSIX specifies that `W*CONTINUED` may not be portable and tells to use `#ifdef` for it (it doesn't for `WIFSTOPPED`. This makes me wonder if there are real systems where we'd be able to determine that a process has been stopped but not continued. Maybe ebd would have to trap SIGCONT there and report this to pkgcore (but that now brings problem of interspersed comms).

Now, if we removed it altogether, I guess pkgcore is going to time out on stopped ebds. Not sure if that will be only a performance concern, or if it will actually cause it to fail in the case.

This is a nice guide to Xen instance types and detection:

http://www.brendangregg.com/blog/2014-05-07/what-color-is-your-xen.html
http://www.brendangregg.com/blog/2014-05-09/xen-feature-detection.html
https://github.com/brendangregg/Misc/blob/master/xen/xen-features.pl

We have instances of both kinds, so I can compare the two here.

If I `cat /sys/hypervisor/properties/features`, HVM returns 00000705, and PV returns 000000f0.

There is no mention of "HVM" in dmesg, so a `dmesg | grep HVM` will return a non-zero errno, that is until the kernel kmsg buffer rotates the boot stuff out.

Running `dmidecode` will return nothing on PV, but will show a BIOS DMI output on HVM:

    docker run --privileged alpine /bin/sh -c 'apk update && apk add dmidecode && dmidecode'

The suggestion to `grep hvm /sys/hypervisor/properties/capabilities` actually returns the same thing for both PV and HVM, so we can't rely on that.

Additionally, we can rely on the meta-data discovery API:

    curl -Ls http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/profile

This will return 'default-paravirtual' for PV, and 'default-hvm' for HVM.

This works on AWS. I'm pretty sure this, or something very similar to this, will also work with OpenStack, Eucalyptus, as well as CloudStack (I think), as they expose the meta-data API at 169.254.169.254. Pretty sure the other cloud platforms do not, but I've not seen anyone do an official poll of this.

So those are the methods we can use.

So to think about this feature, there are a few things I can think of. If this is good, then I'll move to a more formal proposal:

 - break out the authentication API in the controller as its own microservice. It should be pluggable, having the ability to communicate to other auth servers (like LDAP). This'll most likely be pulling out the existing auth service built into the controller and re-implementing it as a standalone service
 - update reference docs, API docs, architecture diagrams etc. to reference this new service
 - lock down auth access through registry v2's [pluggable auth system](https://github.com/docker/distribution/issues/535) by communicating to this new auth microservice. This means we're blocked until we close #3814, since the v1 registry has no pluggable auth service
 - provide a client workflow that wraps `docker push` with the auth server + a call to the controller to create a new build
 - deprecate `deis pull` with this new workflow. This might be just a big fat warning in the client before we remove those endpoints in a future release.

Does that seem reasonable to everyone? :)

@johntzwei Looking a little closer at your error message, it is actually quite odd, expecially this part:

> File "/home/jwei/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/subprocess.py", line 1608, in _try_wait AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'waitpid'

The `_try_wait()` method in Python 3.5 looks like this:

```python
    def _try_wait(self, wait_flags):
        """All callers to this function MUST hold self._waitpid_lock."""
        try:
            (pid, sts) = os.waitpid(self.pid, wait_flags)
        except ChildProcessError:
            # This happens if SIGCLD is set to be ignored or waiting
            # for child processes has otherwise been disabled for our
            # process.  This child is dead, we can't get the status.
            pid = self.pid
            sts = 0
        return (pid, sts)
```

That error would be thrown if the `os` symbol were `None`. The top of the file has `import os`, so something later did something like `subprocess.os = None`. I don't use Anaconda, so it's possible that it did something strange, but I'm not sure what setting `os` to `None` would accomplish. Or perhaps your code reset the `os` symbol on accident?

Anyway, I think I will close this issue soon if the error cannot be reproduced.

# MVCE:

OS: win64

**`.condarc`**
```
channels:
  - defaults
  - https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge

channel_priority: false
```
Note: `channel_priority` is false

**`shuffle.yml`**
```
name: shuffle

channels:
  - defaults

dependencies:
  - gflags
  - libpng
  - zlib <1.2.11
```
-----
Create the environment with an older `zlib`:
```
λ conda env create --file .\shuffle.yml
```

Try to update `zlib`:
```
λ activate shuffle
(shuffle) λ conda update zlib

Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: C:\Miniconda3\envs\shuffle

  added / updated specs:
    - zlib


The following packages will be downloaded:

    package                    |            build
    ---------------------------|-----------------
    zlib-1.2.11                |       h8395fce_2         237 KB

The following packages will be UPDATED:

    gflags: 2.2.1-h6538335_0 --> 2.2.1-vc14_0      conda-forge [vc14]  <-- !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
    libpng: 1.6.30-vc14_1    --> 1.6.34-vc14_0     conda-forge [vc14]  <-- !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
    zlib:   1.2.8-vc14_3     --> 1.2.11-h8395fce_2

Proceed ([y]/n)? y
```

Note the highlighted lines - this is what I believe to be incorrect behaviour (a bug)

The update of a completely unrelated package (`zlib`) has triggered a change in `gflags`.

When I have `channel_priority=false` I only want to update an existing package when there is a greater version available - the channel should play no part in selecting which package to install. Only when installing a *new* package should the `channel_priority` ever be used to break ties.

In this case I have version `2.2.1` of `gflags` installed from the `defaults` channel and the solver wants to replace it with the same version `2.2.1` but from the `conda-forge` channel which is incorrect according to my `channel_priority` setting.

Even if I did have  `channel_priority=true`, `conda-forge` has a lower priority than `defaults` so it still shouldn't choose to replace a package with the same version from a lower priority channel.

After accepting the above changes I now have the new `zlib` *and* the *"UPDATED"* `gflags` and `libpng` so that should be the latest of everything, however if I run an `update --all` conda will choose to revert the changes made to `gflags` and `libpng`:
```
(shuffle) λ conda update --all
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: C:\Miniconda3\envs\shuffle


The following packages will be UPDATED:

    gflags: 2.2.1-vc14_0  conda-forge [vc14] --> 2.2.1-h6538335_0
    libpng: 1.6.34-vc14_0 conda-forge [vc14] --> 1.6.34-h79bbb47_0

Proceed ([y]/n)? y
```

On every single run of conda a whole bunch of packages will be *"UPDATED"* to the other channel causing every change to take a lot more time than it would otherwise.

> Comparing build numbers across channels is meaningless, and I will continue to strongly resist attempts to do so, even though in the past we did so---improperly.

The other way to see it: 

AFAIR there is talk of moving builds from conda-forge to defaults and for that it is meaningful. Basically all situation where one channel is used as source for the other (e.g. conda-forge -> defaults or also debian -> ubuntu), it is meaningful. On the other hand, treating build numbers as not meaningful is only the case where both versions are packaged independently. IMO that's usually, and especially in the future with conda forge as the base, not the case. My guess is that it will happen much more that some people will take conda-forge as upstream, fix an error and push out a fix and then add a PR. That scenario only works with `(upstream, build, channel)` sorting...

From that standpoint (both interpretations are possible, but IMO the latter much more realistic) it's IMO better to treat  `(upstream, build)` as basically `(version)`.

As an historical note, the reason the variable was named `CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV` is because commands such as `conda list`, `conda install`, `conda remove`, etc. have an option `-n` and `-p`, which allow you to be explicit about the conda environment, but they *default* to `CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV`.  I am personally not a fan of using the word "active" or "deactive" as it implied that you have to "activate" an environment before programs in it can be used, which is not the case.

@randomgambit Some of the discussion in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3664 is relevant.  Windows file locking is a P.I.T.A.  That may be what your problem is.  The problem is that virus scanners and auto-backup systems are checking out locks on conda's files before conda is done with them.  Installing larger packages one-at-a-time sometimes helps.  So does (gasp) temporarily disabling your virus scanner.  Obviously, do so at your own risk.

Ultimately, conda just needs to hold the file lock longer and not give it up until it's actually done with the file.

I don't think `conda-build` can/should be used to build software outside of the conda ecosystem. I expect there are too many assumptions made in the codebases for conda and conda-build for that to work (@msarahan, @kalefranz, your thoughts please?), so for Linux and macOS, I would go with the first approach.

On Windows, our package applies a lot of patches that I had to write: https://github.com/mingwandroid/conda-recipes/tree/master/python/rpy2 (note, the first patch *isn't* for Windows but is *probably* fixed in 2.8.3). I've no idea how anyone who builds rpy2 for Windows does it without these fixes. One thing to note is that we use mingw-w64 GCC 5.4 while Rtools uses (its own build of) GCC 4.9.3 so this may be a reason why some of my patches are necessary (but I don't think it is).

The internet is full of tales of woe regarding rpy2 on Windows: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/14882477/rpy2-install-on-windows-7. It is one of the trickier packages I've built (since it involves both MSVC and GCC DLLs) and IMHO on that OS the only sensible course of action is `conda install -c r rpy2`, anything else will be a support nightmare.

If, in this instance, the customer wants to build it against a different (e.g. official) release of R for Windows and wants a conda package for that then it would be a fairly lengthy task, in the order of a week.

As an aside, I would like to get to the bottom of what's missing that they won't use all of our stuff.

@mcg1969

> We have been through this many times internally, I'm afraid, suggesting that it needs to be documented.
> 
> The problem is that if you leave the old package in place, then it's possible for conda to still pick up that broken package with specific combinations of dependencies.

Nothing prevents this in general, so why is the metadata being changed for specific dependencies? This attempts to relieve a package maintenance burden, but in fact makes it more difficult to maintain infrastructure based on and using conda. It is the responsibility of the person depending on `bar` to validate that using `bar` without any version constraint whatsoever works for their package. If it doesn't, that's on them for not being careful about constraining dependencies.

> For instance, suppose package foo 1.0 depends on bar. A new version bar 2.0 is released, and it breaks foo. Nobody knew this at the time foo was built, of course, so they just used a bare, unversioned bar dependency. Unfortunately, this means that if users update bar in those environments, foo is going to break.

If it breaks, then it is the dependent's problem to constrain their dependencies. Currently, an opaque choice is being made for users, rather than forcing people to be conscious of what they are depending on.

> So what we'd like to do is modify the metadata foo 1.0 so that it depends on bar <2.0. None of the program code is changing, this is strictly a metadata change. If we do this for all existing builds, then conda users get the benefit of this new metadata, and their environments will not break if they do conda update bar.

Again, if their environment breaks, it is their responsibility to constrain versions of their dependencies. `conda update` is a red herring because it's completely untenable in a multi-user setting.

> On the other hand, if we refuse to update existing builds, then those builds of foo are now broken in the current conda universe. Anyone who does conda install foo=1.0 bar=2.0, for instance, is going to get the broken build of foo.

Correct me if I'm wrong, but this isn't true if you bump the build number. A user will get an error because they are trying install the latest version of `foo` which is 1.0 which has a *new build number* because of new metadata, and `conda` now knows that `bar` must be less than `2.0` and would fail to install.

I still don't understand why this being special cased for certain packages whose constraints Continuum happens to have knowledge of, and there's absolutely nothing to be done about it.

If the metadata is going to be part of the package, then changes to it must be reflected in the version in *some* way, because the MD5 sum reflects the version of package *including metadata*. Ideally, metadata would be separate from code, but the current system doesn't work like that.

With that in mind bumping the build number seems like the best compromise here.

Windows shell `cmd.exe` works differently than bash. When you run a batch script from one batch script control is given to the child script and the parent is simply stopped. To avoid this and have the child return control to the parent you need to use the command call.

Instead of 
```
conda --version
echo asd
```
you now need to use
```
call conda --version
echo asd
```
otherwise the 2nd line will not be run anymore.

> If a package comes in several variants, the feature-free variant is preferred, unless overridden by an active 'track_features' in the current environment.

No, this isn't the rule. The rule is to _minimize the number of features required to satisfy the specifications. So for instance:

> This is reasonable, but doesn't cover the case when a package has only featured variants.

It _does_ cover this case. If a package only has featured variants, then its feature becomes a _required_ dependency, and the solver will pull in a package with the appropriate `track_features` setting. Which one will it pull in? It's possible this could be ambiguous, if multiple packages offer the `track_features` setting. And certainly, we should document that fact. But you'll always get a valid solution.

> If the second, would it be reasonable to allow other packages to override the 'feature-free is preferred' default as well?

I like the idea of being able to specify a non-feature version of a package. For instance, if you have `mkl` engaged, you might want a non-`mkl` variant of a package. It would be very easy to accomplish this with the solver; we simply need to figure out how to spec it.

> Moreover, what are the implications of three-stage optimization in more difficult situations? For example, when the latest version has no feature-free variant, but an older version has, there is a conflict between the goals 'minimize features' and 'maximize version numbers'.

Earlier stages (e.g., feature count) takes priority over later stages (e.g, version maximization).

Keep in mind: the solver shouldn't necessarily be tasked with handling every possible crazy corner case, as long as it always results in a dependency-compliant install. Much of what we need to do here is to _document_ best practices for using `features` and `track_features`. For instance, we already recommend that `track_features` be embedded in empty metapackages. We violate our own recommendation with `python`, but we are actually working on fixing this in other ways in the future.

As long as the common and reasonable cases work exactly as we expect, and as long as we document those cases where unexpected behavior may be seen, I think we're fine.

Steaming does improve the perception of performance. However, it doesn't actually solve any problem of resolve user issues.
1. The user still has to wait for the list action to finish. The difference is instead of staring at the blank screen he/she watches ever-ending rolling list of information.
2. When the result is large, in many terminals the data still overflow the buffer, causing the results in early porting lost. Doesn't help in streaming.
3. To automation script, there is no difference.

The feature itself is not easy to implement:
1. If the output is table format, the table will be distorted because the width of the columns can't be determined.
2. If the output is JSON format, we need to manually craft the `Array` format.

The original problem the "streaming" feature purpose indents to solve is to improve the usability for commands return a large amount of information while the paging feature is lacking. Let's solve the paging thing actually.

When `get_clouds` is executed in high-parallelism context, there is a high chance it returns nothing. It is because the method will write to the config file first to ensure the known clouds were written and then read the config file again. There isn't lock to prevent and another process from reading the config file during writing eventually cause this issue.

Here's a simple repo demonstrate the root cause of this issue:
```Python
import os.path
import configparser
import multiprocessing
import sys

def init_clouds(config_file):
    config = configparser.ConfigParser()
    config.read(config_file)
    with open(config_file, 'w') as write_to:
        config.write(write_to)


def load_config(id):
    config_file = '/Users/troydai/.azure/clouds.config'
    init_clouds(config_file)

    if not os.path.exists(config_file):
        raise Exception('no file') 
    
    parser = configparser.ConfigParser()
    parser.read(config_file)
    sys.stdout.write(str(len(parser.sections())))

p = multiprocessing.Pool(2)
p.map(load_config, range(10000))
p.close()

p.join()
```

There is a larger implication here. It seems to me that we're frequently writing content to the config file. For each command execution, the same content is written to the configure file over and over and essentially erases many user settings. There are some modification users make will never take effect because of the force writing in every command.

Hi @charliejllewellyn 
This one I can answer :)

When you call a RestAPI, you need to specify an API-Version. It's a requirement of Azure, there is no default ApiVersion. However, the SDK does this for you, for simple scenario on public Azure (but you can configure that of course).

Now, when you call an operation, the object you give as parameter changes depending of the ApiVersion. This is known as "models", meaning when you call an operation with a specific ApiVersion, you need to use the model from the same ApiVersion to talk to Azure. Otherwise there is mismatch and your request will be refused.

The specific error you got, is when you called an operation with the non-default ApiVersion, but using the default model from public Azure. I know the message is impossible to understand, I'm working to make this more explicit...

When you (or Ansible) do:
```python
import azure.mgmt.compute.models
```
You don't specifiy an ApiVersion, so you get the default models definition (the public Azure ones). Then you **can't** use this syntax if you customize the ApiVersion of the client.

On the package used by Ansible right now, you need  to get models using the `models` class method of the client:
```python
models = azure.mgmt.compute.ComputeManagementCliemt.models("2015-06-15")
vm = models.VirtualMachine(...)
```

It's a little cumbersome, since you defined the ApiVersion twice: one for the client, and one for the `models`. Then, and recent packages (since compute 3.1.0rc1, storage 1.5.0 or network 1.6.0), you can define it only once:
```python
client = azure.mgmt.compute.ComputeManagementClient(credentials, subscription_id, api_version="2015-06-15")
vm_models = client.virtual_machines.models
vm = vm_models.VirtualMachine(...)
```

This ensure you that the models are in sync with operation you use.

FYI @tstringer If Ansible is still using `import azure.mgmt.compute.models`, this doesn't scale to AzureStack

> added one module usage limitation

I still think this is a bad move. Yes, this feature will allow us to have nice hello-world examples. Yes, this feature will make simple to make single modules web applications as like it was for single page web apps. But. If application will grow and user will be forced to split it into modules he/she will have to rewrite whole the routing system, application initialization and drop global context usage. And all this may not be quite oblivious. So there will be two chairs problem: or stay within  a single module forever or cry for help.

Even for small applications, decorator-way may not be convenient. I'm working now on service which have only two routes. But, these routes are belongs to different API versions and I don't want to mix them or their logic in a single module because of coupling problem. So they are defined in two different modules, each of those defines how each API version should act.

There will be also a problem to use different routers with such kind of design. For instance, [aiohttp_traversal](https://github.com/zzzsochi/aiohttp_traversal). Decorator routes cannot be used with this one by design reasons.

I think there's definitely a race problem of some sort; doing a GET followed too quickly by a POST gives various errors, typically `Can not write request body for ...`, or sometimes `[Errno 32] Broken pipe`.

Inserting a short `await asyncio.sleep()` — as short as 0.001 seconds, in some cases — between them fixes the problem. Doing an `await request.text()` doesn't seem to help, however.

@Lukasa I deeply respect your reasons for not wanting to go down this path with certify. Your module is beautifully simple as is and fits its designated purpose perfectly. Handling native libraries is a very different proposition.

Similarly, I too look forward to PEP543 getting up and providing mechanisms to handling issues with native TLS verification in a cleaner way.

In the mean time however I've been tasked with simplifying the setup of python on windows machines at work and configuring them to use an internal devpi server which uses an internally managed CA. 
This CA is automatically installed on every windows machine in the company automatically by domain policy. This doesn't help in python though...

As certifi is in the enviable position of being respected by requests (both the separate module and the copy bundled with pip) it's in a great position to transparently handle this issue. 

To meet my goals without needing to compromise yours, I rolled these changes by @rodcloutier into a new module that patches certifi at runtime. 
The mechanisms have been fixed slightly and now only re-run the pem export when needed, rather than at every startup.
The runtime patching does not directly touch the installed certifi module, it leverages wrapt and a framework based on autowrapt to modify certifi in memory the first time its imported.

https://pypi.python.org/pypi/python-certifi-win32

I do hope you don't see this as disrespecting your certifi module. 
I agree it's a bit of a nasty hack but it's very convenient in practice.

(3) is only tricky for the case that the Kernel object hasn't yet been instantiated since the kernel queue ought to handle it without telling it anything about cells. A slow-starting kernel does not affect when the Kernel is instantiated, it only affects when the kernel_ready event fires and true execution can begin. Currently, the Kernel object is instantiated as soon as the session REST API returns, which is quite prompt. If we could either:

0. instantiate the Kernel object immediately, since it's useful immediately now, or
0. accept the longstanding behavior for the much shorter period between load and Kernel existing, or
0. disable execution (clearly) for the very brief time until the kernel exists

then the easy to follow queue on the kernel would be all we need.

I have found another bug, in [ `Cmaker.has_cmake_cache_arg`](https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/master/skbuild/cmaker.py#L60-L70), which would cause the tests to fail: since it traverses the list of parameters in order, `has_cmake_cache_arg(['-DOVERRIDE:STRING=A', '-DOVERRIDE:STRING=B'], 'OVERRIDE', 'B')` returns `False`, while the way command line arguments are parsed, `OVERRIDE` should be set to `'B'`.
The simple solution here is replacing the [iteration line](https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build/blob/master/skbuild/cmaker.py#L64) with `for arg in reversed(cmake_args):`, which will check that the last occurrence of `arg_name` is set to `arg_value`.

How about this for this sprint?
* Subdomains can only be created by their domain names.
* Any domain name can broadcast a subdomain zone file update, but not an address change.
* Only the original domain name can send an address change, and it will only be considered valid if the domain has all of the previous zone files up to that point.

We can worry about `care_of` and on-chain recovery in the next sprint.

Hi @stiell, I have done some initial work on this.  You are right--we want to stop attackers from uploading old versions of the profiles.  We didn't go with timestamps at first since we were worried about clock skew, but in relying on a signature over the previous hash, we were assuming that there is a data store holding your last profile that will correctly serve it on request.  Normally this is the case--the client keeps a copy on your computer.  But as you point out, this leads to problems if there is inconsistency across the set of replicas.  This will be fixed in 0.14.

# Toil Recompute

The goal of the Toil recompute was to process ~20,000 RNA-seq samples to create a consistent meta-analysis of four datasets free of computational batch effects. Using Toil, UCSC's pipeline architecture, we ran this recompute in a little over 3 days on a large AWS cluster for a cost of $1.30 per sample.

## Datasets
We chose unaligned RNA samples from 4 datasets

1. The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)
2. Genotype-Tissue Expression (GTEx)
3. Therapeutically Applicable Research To Generate Effective Treatments (TARGET)
4. Pacific Pediatric Neuro-Oncology Consortium (PNOC)

[Dataset Image]

## Pipeline
The RNA-seq CGL pipeline is shown below.  CutAdapt was used for adapter trimming, STAR was used for alignment, and RSEM and Kallisto were used as quantifiers. 

[DAG Image]

#### Pipeline Inputs
STAR, RSEM, and Kallisto indexes were all built with the same reference genome and annotation file.  

* Reference genome:  HG38 (no alt analysis) with overlapping genes from the PAR locus removed ( chrY:10,000-2,781,479 and chrY:56,887,902-57,217,415 ).  
    * ftp://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genomes/archive/old_genbank/Eukaryotes/vertebrates_mammals/Homo_sapiens/GRCh38/seqs_for_alignment_pipelines
* Annotation: Gencode V23 comprehensive annotation (CHR)
    * http://www.gencodegenes.org/releases/23.html)

Inputs were generated with the following commands
* STAR
    * `sudo docker run -v $(pwd):/data quay.io/ucsc_cgl/star --runThreadN 32 --runMode genomeGenerate --genomeDir /data/genomeDir --genomeFastaFiles hg38.fa --sjdbGTFfile gencode.v23.annotation.gtf`
* Kallisto
    * `sudo docker run -v $(pwd):/data quay.io/ucsc_cgl/kallisto index -i hg38.gencodeV23.transcripts.idx transcriptome_hg38_gencodev23.fasta`
* RSEM
    * `sudo docker run -v $(pwd):/data --entrypoint=rsem-prepare-reference jvivian/rsem -p 4 --gtf gencode.v23.annotation.gtf hg38.fa hg38`

Sadly, I can only +1 @nerdvegas comment rather than the +100 it deserves. Assigning to `_` at the top of a function/method is an invaluable way to silence lint warnings about unused parameters while communicating to the human reader that the author intentionally meant to not use the parameter. It is an extremely useful convention. The alternative is a magic comment to silence the linter about the unused parameter. But I fail to see how that is any different from assigning to `_`.

Looking a little more closely, there seems to be a typo in the code for LineProxyList's _generate_expected_list() that should deal with this scenario. 

Specifically the code block: 
```
if previous and previous.type == "endl" and i[0].type != "endl" and previous.indentation != indentation:
                log("Previous is endl and current isn't endl and identation isn't correct, fix it")
                previous.indent = indentation
```
In this context "indentation" is the desired indentation for the current code block.
"previous" is the immediately prior node and "previous.indentation" is a property that looks up the number of trailing spaces on the endl separator prior to previous (unless previous was an "endl" in which previous.indentation is None). i[0] is the node we are operating on. 

In the assignment, previous.indent -- i.e. the number of spaces following the endl is set to the proper 
indentation, and the log message seems to imply that this is fixing up an indentation mismatch. But it is assigning previous.indent whereas the test in the if statement is on previous.indentation. 

I think the conditional is a typo and was intended to be "previous.indent != indentation". 

Fixing this (patch below) solves this issue. In addition, all of the testcases in the source tree pass. 
```
--- base_nodes.py.orig	2017-01-02 15:59:58.000000000 -0600
+++ base_nodes.py	2017-11-16 07:45:47.716985388 -0600
@@ -1746,7 +1757,7 @@
             expected_list.append(i[0])
             log("-- current result: %s", ["".join(map(lambda x: x.dumps(), expected_list))])
 
-            if previous and previous.type == "endl" and i[0].type != "endl" and previous.indentation != indentation:
+            if previous and previous.type == "endl" and i[0].type != "endl" and previous.indent != indentation:
                 log("Previous is endl and current isn't endl and identation isn't correct, fix it")
                 previous.indent = indentation

```

@Ichimonji10 I guess my single biggest concern is that the default results per page is 20. This could result in a number of failed searches, especially when dealing with automation. For example, over the lifecycle of the machines we currently run our automation on, there are Organizations with id's exceeding 530! Of course, a portion of these are deleted but most remain. For most non-automated users, they may never encounter this problem. But if we are running automation and are searching for an organization we just created, there is a high probability that the search will fail.

There are two main solutions to this issue. 
1. Hand it off to the person running the search. This would basically mean that we need to include a large page count in each search query. That is fine, but maybe not optimal.
2. Nailgun defaults to a per_page that is large enough to return a significant number of results. This helps to ensure our target is included in the results, but can quickly bog down a nailgun-based search. 

Of course, it would be optimal if the person performing the search includes a server-side filter. Just my thoughts.

There seems to be a bit of confusion on just what the error is being generated.

According to the output, the error is:

```
[Errno 42] Protocol not available
```

This looks like ENOPROTOOPT to me. I believe this is correct on Mac OS X, where ENOPROTOOPT is 42. See https://developer.apple.com/library/ios/documentation/System/Conceptual/ManPages_iPhoneOS/man2/intro.2.html

However, often ENOPROTOOPT is defined as 92, not 42. e.g. on my Linux system.

```
Python 2.7.8 (default, Oct 18 2014, 12:50:18) 
[GCC 4.9.1] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import errno
>>> errno.ENOPROTOOPT
92
```

If Python incorrectly comes with ENOPROTOOPT set to 92, instead of 42, and it gets 42 instead, it may crash like this, as the exception handler won't work.

```python
    try:
        ...
        sockaddr_in = sock.getsockopt(socket.SOL_IP,
                                      SO_ORIGINAL_DST, SOCKADDR_MIN)
        ...
    except socket.error, e:
        if e.args[0] == errno.ENOPROTOOPT:
            return sock.getsockname()
        raise
```

Please try running the following commands on Mac OS X, and let me know what number it gives:

```
# python
import errno
errno.ENOPROTOOPT
```

If me theory is correct, changing "if e.args[0] == errno.ENOPROTOOPT:" to "if e.args[0] == 42:" will fix the problem - although unfortunately will break on other platforms.

Thanks

@wojtek0806 @zancas I said this before in the Slack channel but i think it's a bad idea to specifically list the versions of TMOS that the SDK supports...specifically for the reason that this issue points out.

I had said before that we should instead be comparing greater-than and less-than using StrictVersion because of situations _exactly_ like this one; people using the SDK on versions we haven't yet tried it on.

It's more appropriate to do that and list it in the documentation that this is what we support than it is to hard-code it like this in the SDK.

Another point is that when the SDK is inevitably abandoned, all future versions of BIG-IP will be unable to use it. At that point the customers will, yet again, need to maintain things or abandon them and start over...again.

It's more practical to specify a minimum_version in the base and then override that in all classes where that minimum is no longer valid. Then use StrictVersion to check that.

I've used this approach before, and while it does work quite well, I have since found an important caveat: Django drops and recreates content types for the models. @joohoi I can confirm that separating them out makes it work perfectly (although this is a change since the last major release). Can this be documented somewhere unless I missed it previously.

For other users to be clearer:

* It's important to separate all vhost definitions into separate files. This means the port 80 and 443 ports into separate conf files
* So if you have one vhosts file such as mysite.conf
```
<VirtualHost *:80>
  ServerAlias www.mysite.com
  ServerName mysite.com
</VirtualHost>
<VirtualHost *:443>
    ServerName mysite.com
    ServerAlias www.mysite.com
</VirtualHost>
```

Then you want to convert this to
mysite.conf
```
<VirtualHost *:443>
    ServerName mysite.com
    ServerAlias www.mysite.com
</VirtualHost>
```

and 80-mysite.conf
```
<VirtualHost *:80>
  ServerAlias www.mysite.com
  ServerName mysite.com
</VirtualHost>
```

@gelin I disagree with the general principle that libraries should not crash on the event of malformed data. Otherwise, you end up silently coercing errors and downstream users have no way of finding out that their input data is malformed. Ideally, an upstream library like `dateutil` would provide an error that could be caught, and hopefully some way to recover from it.

The problem here is that `rrulestr` is parsing into an opaque structure, so there's no way to recover from this particular error without re-writing most of the `rrulestr` parser as a fallback for the main parser.

Possible options:
1. Expose the components of the `rrule` so that a user can conditionally modify the rule based on what gets parsed.
2. Expose a method like `rrulestr.to_kwargs` that parses the rrule into a `dict` that can be passed to `rrule`, allowing for modifications to the parsed components before construction.

I also think that this should fail during construction, not during evaluation of the rule. If we go that route, option 1 (which is more work anyway) is infeasible, since it would be impossible to create an `rrule` that could be modified anyway. Plus, option 1 would mean we'd have to expose the components of *all* `rrule`s, which isn't a *terrible* idea but it's a bit broader than the solution to this problem, so I think the right way to go is option 2 where an error is raised on mixture of timezone-naive and timezone-aware datetimes during construction.

I checked your example in #125 and I don't see what you couldn't do with `attribute`.

```python
class UserSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.String(deserialize_from="Name")
    email_addr = fields.String(attribute="email", deserialize_from="EmailAddress")
```

could be written

```python
class UserSchema(Schema):
    Name = fields.String(attribute='name')
    EmailAddress = fields.String(attribute='email')
```

However, `attribute` doesn't solve to your example above:

```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    ApiKey = fields.String(attribute='apikey')
    weird-key = fields.String(attribute='weird_key')  # syntax error
```

This could be solved on user side with meta-programming, but it sucks.

Maybe this is the reason `attribute` didn't work for you.

A symmetric to `attribute` would allow this (let's call it `key`):

```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    apikey = fields.String(key='apikey')
    weird_key = fields.String(key='weird-key')
```
and in fact, if it existed, it would cover all that `attribute` does so `attribute` itself wouldn't be useful, would it?

This would allow dump/load to/from any API with keys that are invalid python keywords on a symmetric fashion (load key = dump key) and with unique keys (only one key accepted for a field).

What `load_from` / `dump_to` brings on top of this is

- asymmetry
- different keys allowed

If this is correct, then it boils down to whether we want asymmetry (from what we said, different schemas sounds better) and whether we want to allow multiple keys for a field (this sounds wrong).

Should we implement `attribute`'s symmetric parameter? How should it be named? Should we keep `attribute`, then?

We've added a safeguard in v.50b3 that prevents from creating an automatic validation test if the dataset is too small. The reason, as I have mentioned above, is to try to prevent the case that both train and test sets contain only part of the labels (motions).   
I admit this decision is done rather crudely - we simply don't allow splitting if there's less than a certain amount of sessions (currently hard coded to 100).  

In addition - the safeguard was added mainly to prevent the `activity_classifier.create()` method function from creating an auto validation set. In no way it should block a user who is explicitly calling the `random_split_by_session()` API. 

I'm changing this issue to a feature request, so we will add:
1. As a first step - an enforce flag to `random_split_by_session()`, allowing the user to split his sessions on any dataset when using this API.
2. We should probably improve the decision mechanism for whether or not to create a validation set during `create()`. It should check the labels distribution of the train and validation sets (or some other method) - rather then a hard limit on the number of sessions.

@yiheng We have considered about __pipeline-style__ API, and we have tried  _Transformer_ API of BigDL. After some trails, we found that __pipeline__ is less convenient than __DAG__. 

And then we have tried to build  DAGOperators/DAGScheduler on the basis of __Transformer__. It's far more friendly than __pipeline__. I can show a small sample here:
      
    val rows: Iterator[Row] = (1 to 10).map(_ => randRow(3, 20, 3)).toIterator
    val denseFea1 = ToDenseTensor[Float](ColumnSelector[Float]((3 to 12).toArray), 2)
    val denseFea2 = TensorOp[Float](Tensor[Float](randFloats(5), Array(1, 5)))
    val sparseFea1 = KVToTensor[Float]("sparse1", 100)
    val sparseFea2 = KVToTensor[Float]("sparse2", 100)
    val lbl = ToDenseTensor[Float](ColumnSelector[Float](13))
    val feature = DAGResult(dim = 2) ++=
      (Seq(denseFea1, denseFea2, sparseFea1, sparseFea2): _*)
    val label = DAGResult(dim = 2) += lbl
    val r2s: Transformer[Row, Sample[Float]] = RowToSample[Float](feature, label)

We build a tiny __DAG__ in a __Transformer__ named __RowToSample__.

After that, we found data pre-processing procedure is still isolated from main model, which means users need to define it separately, apart from main model. 
But, in my opinion, in most of the time, users just want a single DAG to define the whole procedure rather than distinguishing data pre-processing steps from main model. 
For instance, _hash_embedding_ operation contains a pre-processing action(hashing a string to an index) and a network layer(LookupTable). In _Tensorflow_, users can use __tf.feature_column__  API to do jobs like this easily.  

After reading codes about __Graph__ and __Operation__,  I think maybe we can provide a similar way to define the procedure like _Tensorflow_ by them? Maybe I can add  an __Activity__ wrapper for __spark.sql.Row__ as a start point of the whole procedure(graph)?

3. Compile the produced C program on Android.

Yes, I imply that the signature used internally isn't suitable for concurrency like this. 
Recall that the signature of tactics is of the form "goal x transforms -> goal list x transforms".
The "and-then" tactic composes two tactics with such signatures and returns a tactic with 
the same signature "goal x transforms -> goal list x transforms".
Suppose you want to compose with a third tactic, it would wait until all subgoals are returned from the and-then tactic, then it would be able to operate. You could create an ad-hoc par-then that would work on a list of tactics, but it would not be able to consume subgoals created by a or-else tactic or similar. 
I claim it would require a more substantial design to understand what should work.
It is basically a matter of using a suitable (continuation) monad where the nasty business 
includes dealing properly with tactic failures and protection and contention on shared state.

> What I found weird is that there is no upcast if alpha is a normal python float, only when is np.float32.

Yes, that's because numpy and Theano have different upcasting rules when dealing with Python floats or Python ints. Note that the leaky rectifier formula (similar to the dropout layer and the update rules) include constants that the code originally had as Python floats or ints. Now we run into the following discrepancy:
* Python float + Python float: Python float
* Python float + numpy float32: float64
* Python float + Theano float32: float32

> I'm genuinely confused with the different cases for upcasting.

They're somewhat unexpected, that's why we didn't see the problems in the first place! They only creep up if you supply a numpy array for the dropout rate (or leakiness, or beta), not if you use a Python float or a Theano variable.

  "path": "example-notebooks",
  "created": "2018-01-24T17:47:24.918265Z",

* `/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/unicorn/lib/libunicorn.dylib`
* `/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/simuvex/lib/sim_unicorn.dylib`

* `sudo install_name_tool -change libpyvex.dylib /Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/pyvex/lib/libpyvex.dylib /Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/simuvex/lib/sim_unicorn.dylib`

On Wed, 21 Jun 2017, AhmedRejeb wrote:

> hi, my name is Ahmed, and I'm working on a Pixhawk with a raspberry board,
> I developed a PID tuning loop on Simulink that have as output the PWM signal
> for 4 motors
> I want to use the DroneKit -python lib to send those messages (PWMs) to the
> Pixhawk to control the motor but I don't know how can you help me

You will almost certainly find that the latencies mean flight will not 
be stable.  Consider ArduCopter's stabilization loops happen at 400Hz - 
that gives you 2.5millisecondss to get data to simulink, calculate the PWM 
outputs and return them to ArduCopter - that's really not going to happen 
from a latency perspective!

Additionally, I do not believe ArduPilot has the ability to source PWM 
information for the motors from any external source - however, you could 
notionally introduce a new GUIDED mode which takes information from a 
newly-crafted mavlink packet which sets the relevant outputs.

I can't talk to PX4 Pro's abilities to source motor PWM externally.

One option you may have is to talk directly to a simulator; there is a 
clear boundary between simulators and ArduPilot - sensor data from 
simulator to ArduPilot, pwm outputs from ArduPilot to simulator.  You may 
be able to use that to eliminate the autopilot altogether.  ArduPilot's
in-tree simulators are not the only ones with such clearly defined 
boundaries.

https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/SITL/SIM_Aircraft.cpp#L325
https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/SITL/SIM_Aircraft.h#L39

Using the ArduPilot simulator has the benefit of being able to 
lock-step-schedule to debug your code.

> coordinates be used, but these datasets aren't projected to a lon/lat grid.
> At least not always. The x/y coordinates for these datasets are the X/Y
> metered coordinates in the projected coordinate system. These coordinates
> are uniform for each col/row they describe so the coordinates are only 1D
> arrays along their given axis. For lon/lat coordinates we would have to
> specify the values for every coordinate in the .coords attribute of the
> DataArray. Since xarray requires coordinates to be computed (not lazy dask
> arrays) this would take up a large amount of memory.
